A love letter gets used only once, and if it doesn't land, you can clarify it in play. Print the love letter out or write it on an index card, or send it to the player online. During play, have them read the letter aloud and do what it says. If you write love letters for multiple PCs, you can set them up to be read in a specific order, or let the players choose who goes when. Take notes! Incorporate the results into this and future sessions. Make the results and their choices matter. Some examples: [!love letter] Dear Rhianna, You got back from a few days hunting to find everyone talking about Caradoc, Vahid, and their trip to the Tower. As if you didn't have enough to worry about. Speaking of worries, roll +Fortunes: on a 10+, pick 1; on a 7-9, pick 2; on a 6-, mark XP and all 3. - Owain's been calling for folks to "do something" about Vahid—who has been agreeing with him, that you would not have expected? - Wini (Cerys's eldest) has been acting out, badly—how so? - Crinwin jumped one of your crew while out hunting (who?); they're home, but badly hurt (how so?) XOXO – your GM [!love letter] Dear Caradoc, You and Morwenna are still tied up, but you're at the greenhouse. Tell us what unpleasant truth you had to reveal to seal the deal, then roll +CHA: on a 10+, pick 2; on a 7-9, pick 1; on a 6-, pick 1, mark XP, and your captors clearly know they're being followed—they're laying a trap! - You've slyly loosened your bonds - Morwenna's calm & playing along (else, she's freaking right out) - They haven't separated you and Morwenna yet (else, they have) XOXO – your GM [!love letter] Dear Vahid, It's been a quiet, cozy winter, with plenty of time inside. What unlikely aspect of a personality has the Mindgem revealed, now that you've spent so much time with it? Now, ask the Mindgem a question about the Makers, their history, or their arts. You've gleaned a clear and useful answer, and even a few follow-up questions. But also, roll +WIS: on a 10+, you've not neglected anyone nor anything; on a 7-9, tell us who you've let down in some little way; on a 6-, mark XP, and tell us who you've let down and why they're still upset. XOXO – your GM [!love letter] Dear Blodwen (read after Rhianna), Dawn finds you all hiding in a cart, rattling out into the fields after a sleepless night tending to Tierny and the others (with Brin's able help). What personal advice did you give Brin before you left? As for Tierny, roll +INT. If Rhianna's turn led to casualties, then roll with disadvantage. On a 10+, Tierny has made a full recovery and few will know how close she was to death; on a 7-9, she's up and will recover, but your name will spread as a miracleworker; on a 6-, either she dies and becomes a martyr, or lives on as a husk of her former self (your call). XOXO – your GM

830.title: The Game Ongoing
830.body: In this chapter --- --- Ending a session Between sessions Starting a session Pacing Advancement Retiring PCs New PCs Conflict between PCs Time skips Shake-ups Ending your game

831.title: Ending a session
831.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Ending a session

832.title: Do the move
832.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Ending a session › Do the move
832.body: Make time for the End of Session move each time you play. Start to wrap up the action 30 minutes before folks need to leave, and give yourselves at least 15 minutes to do the End of Session move. Set a timer. It's important. Take notes! Not just the wishes, but all of it: the times where each PC engaged with their instinct, the changes to relationships, what the group learned and accomplished, and what each player praised. These notes are invaluable between sessions, as you reflect and try to recall what happened. The last two steps of the move—praise and wishes—are the most important. They allow you to calibrate the game and create space for everyone to give feedback. If the move reveals a larger issue—like a conflict between players or dissatisfaction with the game—then do a check-in . If it involves the whole group and you've all got time and space, then do it now. If that's not practical, or just one or two players are involved, then take it as a follow-up to discuss the issue later.

833.title: END OF SESSION
833.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › END OF SESSION
833.body: When a session ends , point out how you demonstrated or struggled with your instinct. If you can, mark XP. Say how your relationship with or opinion of a PC, NPC, or group has changed. If you can, mark XP. Answer these questions as a group. For each "yes," everyone marks XP. - Did we learn more about the world or its history? - Did we defeat a threat to Stonetop or the region? - Did we improve our standing with our neighbors? - Did we make a lasting improve ment to Stonetop, or tangible progress towards doing so? Praise something about the session (in the fiction or around the table) that you enjoyed or appreciated. Finally, offer up a wish for future sessions: more \ \ , less \ \ , a chance to \ \ , handling \ \ in a different way, etc. Wishes can be about what happens in the fiction or around the table. The GM will take notes. ^end-of-session For more guidance, see End of Session of the Player Moves chapter.

834.title: Handle logistics
834.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › END OF SESSION › Handle logistics
834.body: Schedule the next session if you can. If not, pick a tentative date or two and make plans to confirm. Games die more often from scheduling neglect than from lack of interest. Identify follow-up tasks. Who needs to do what between now and next time? Assign responsibilities and when they need to be done by. For example: - All: Confirm availability (tomorrow) - Hobbes: Pick date for next session, send invites (following day) - Jamie: Level Up (by next session) - Jeremy: Make a Plan for Vahid's gizmo (by next session) - All: Post goals for winter (by Friday) Ask one of your players to track these tasks and follow up, ensuring that they get done. You've got enough to juggle. Let someone else do the badgering. Finally, help the host clean up. It's basic courtesy, and shows respect for the space and the people you're sharing it with.

835.title: Take notes
835.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › END OF SESSION › Take notes
835.body: What happened this session that you don't want to forget? What are your next steps? Write that stuff down. Now. While it's still fresh in your mind. You don't need to write a full session recap right now (it's late, you're probably tired). But try to capture: - Your personal follow-up tasks ("Make a Plan for Vahid's gizmo," or "Talk to Bob about kibitzing") - NPCs you introduced and their key details ("Stamat: sadistic mercenary; flamboyant; scarred, dead eye") - Important decisions you made on the fly, especially those not yet revealed in play ("Callum is dead" or "Nanzl knows Brynmor is lying") - Consequential events that you want to make relevant in future sessions ("Nanzl got a lock of Kios's hair," or "Sawyl is pissed at Nolwen"). - What you had in mind when you dropped a hint or introduced some provocative detail ("Silver goo is sign of the corrupted hagr," or "Una's fiery gem = heart of the Mighty Servant") - Decisions you know you need to make ("What is in Piotr's cellar?") - Prep you know you'll want to do ("Draw a map of the barrow")

836.title: Between sessions
836.body: Use the time between sessions to tend to the game: to reflect, take notes, prep, and make plans.

837.title: Reflection
837.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Reflection
837.body: Remember: GMing is a practice . You get better at it by doing it, and by trying to do better next time. Spend time after each session reflecting on the previous one. Ask yourself: - What goals had you set for yourself? Did you meet them? If not, why not? - What went well? What are you proud of? What did your players praise? - What did your players wish for? How can you make those wishes come true? - Where did the game falter or drag? Where did you feel uncertain? What would you do differently, in retrospect? - Did you pursue your agenda ? Did you follow your principles ? If not, how was the game impacted? What would it have looked like if you had? You might do this formally, sitting down and taking notes. Or maybe you just think about it while you commute or do chores. However you go about it, reflect on the session. Don't beat yourself up, but be honest. Consider what worked and what didn't. Think about why, and about how you can do better. Write down 1-3 goals for the next session. As you prep, keep these goals in mind. During the session, put them somewhere you can see them. Try to meet them.

838.title: Session recap(s)
838.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Session recap(s)
838.body: Every session should get a written recap, an official history of what happened. Few people can recall the events of a session a few days later, much less after weeks or months. So make sure the key moments of each session get written down. If one of your players takes session notes, then ask them to post those notes somewhere public. Review them before you start prepping for the next session, and make corrections or additions as needed. You might find it helpful (or necessary) to make a session recap yourself. Maybe your players don't take notes, or their notes are so detailed that the other players will never read them. Maybe they leave out stuff that you think is important. There's no one right way to do this. Some groups like a detailed, scene-by-scene breakdown. Others prefer a bullet-point of key events, like this:

839.title: Caradoc and Vahid:
839.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Session recap(s) › Caradoc and Vahid:
839.body: - Went to Ruined Tower - Ghost stories at the Crossroads! - Explored outer ruins, got makerglass shard and bronze plaque w/spell - Beznpol attacked; took its hide

840.title: Blodwen:
840.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Session recap(s) › Blodwen:
840.body: - Big public fight with Tegwen, renounces her as kin - Moves out, moves in with Seren - Tegwen is petty & mean (surprise!) - Lewela visits, tearful support Rhianna (not present, off hunting?) Do whatever works for your table, but make sure a running record of play exists, and that it's available to all the players.

841.title: Bookkeeping
841.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Bookkeeping
841.body: Pull out your notes, the steading playbook, your threat tracker—whatever tools you're using—and bring them up to date. - 1) Update your threats , following the procedure on Updating. - 2) Update your NPCs , wherever you track them. Cross off those who've died, update notes for existing ones, and add new NPCs from the previous session. See Creating NPCs ) for guidance. - 3) Update followers. If you expect an NPC to start acting as a follower, then write them up using the steps on Creating followers. Then review and update any existing followers, per Updating followers. - 4) Update the steading playbook , to reflect changes to Fortunes, Surplus, debilities, resources, fortifications, and assets. Mark progress made towards improvements. - 5) Review content guidelines and update them if needed, based on player discussions and your own needs. If you realize that the guidelines need to change, or that your group has been deviating from them, check in with the players Check-ins or make a note to do so. - 6) Review any loot the PCs acquired. Make sure it's on someone's inventory insert or otherwise accounted for. If you need to provide players handouts or write-ups, make a note to do so. If you haven't revealed what the loot is or does, consider how you will—see "Identifying artifacts" and "Identifying arcana" . 7) Update your "I wonder…" list ). If you've answered any of these questions, then jot the answer down and cross off the question. Have any of the questions changed, evolved, gained clarity or nuance? Update them. Have any new questions occurred to you? Add them to the list.

842.title: Follow-ups
842.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Follow-ups
842.body: As the session ended, you likely found yourself with some follow-up tasks. As you reflect and take care of bookkeeping, you'll likely identify more. Review your to-do list and prioritize: - Start with time-sensitive tasks, stuff that needs to happen before the next session (like confirming availability, proposing a big retcon, or reviewing a player's special request). - Next, focus on tasks relevant to the upcoming session (like making a key decision about a threat, or writing up an artifact that the PCs acquired). - For everything else, work on them only if you feel inspired to do so and you know you have time. Otherwise, keep them on your to-do list until they become timely. Don't Make a Plan now for a player whose PC will be busy for the next few sessions.

843.title: Prep
843.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Prep
843.body: As the next session approaches, make time to sit down and prep. Think about where things left off last session. Where are the PCs and what are they up to? What do you expect to happen in the next session? Prep for that. If the PCs are… - … embarking on a journey , then see the What to prep section of the Expeditions chapter What to prep. - … already on a journey , review your prep. Where are the PCs? What are the next legs of travel, points of interest, and challenges? Which encounters are most likely to come up in the next session? Flesh them out if you need to. - … about to explore a site , then prep it to whatever level of detail you find useful and have time for. See Creating sites on Creating sites. - … exploring a site already , what are they likely to encounter next? Flesh that out if you need to. - … likely to face any dangers or make any discoveries , then prep those if you have not already. Find stuff from Book II and review those entries, or make your own. For guidance, see: - Preparing hazards - Monsters - Clues - Encounters - Opportunities - Creating artifacts Artifacts]] - Minor arcana - Major arcana - … likely to interact with NPCs , review your notes on them. If you think you'll introduce new NPCs, see Creating NPCs for guidance. - … likely to gain a new follower , then find their stats in Book II or make them from scratch Creating followers. - … heading home or into a stretch of downtime , then prep for a homefront session. See " What to prep" on What to prep of the Homefront chapter.

844.title: Other things you might prep
844.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Prep › Other things you might prep
844.body: - An adventure hook to kick off a new adventure Wishes and personal goals - Custom moves to use in a specific situation. - Love letters to use at the start of a session. - Setup questions like those you asked at the start of the First Adventure, as a way to warm players up, establish details, tie back to prior events, or foreshadow future ones. - Start of session moves. For example, if you have a Would-be Hero with the Destined background, prep a vision or omen for them! - Specific scenes you'd like to play out: their purpose, descriptions, lines of NPC dialogue, GM moves, etc. - Play aids like maps, visuals, player handouts like arcana cards, etc. - Stuff to support player wishes and your personal goals .

845.title: Adventure hooks
845.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Prep › Adventure hooks
845.body: If the last session ended in downtime, you might want to kick off a new adventure. Maybe the Seasons Changed and you owe the players an opportunity or threat. Maybe they got a 7-9 to Pull Together and choose "unforeseen cost, requirement, or challenge." Maybe the Ranger tried to Persuade Gorlas to leave that haunted ruin alone, but rolled a 4, and so old Gorlas he ain't gonna listen. To prep for a new adventure, follow the process laid out in the First Adventure chapter First Adventure. Or, if it makes sense, prep a full-on crisis instead.

846.title: Wishes and personal goals
846.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Prep › Wishes and personal goals
846.body: Look over the wishes that your players gave you at the end of the last session, or any unmet wishes from before that. Look over the goals you wrote down as part of your reflection , or write 1-3 goals for yourself now—things you'd like to work on, do better, or just remember to do. It's fine (ideal, even) if these overlap with your players' wishes. For each wish or goal, what would help you achieve it? Work that into your prep! This could mean: - Prepping a specific hook, site, danger, etc., or prepping it in a certain way - Making a list, reminder, or cheat sheet and keeping it somewhere visible - Writing a to-do list for the session, with scenes to frame, GM moves to make, questions to ask, etc. - Writing a custom move to handle a specific situation - Brainstorming possible impressions, GM moves, bits of NPC dialogue, ways to resolve player moves, etc. During the End of Session, Vahid's wish is for a lead on completing the Mighty Servant. So between sessions, I prep a hook: a traveler selling an old scroll that describes a fiery glass "heart," powering a device in the fallen city of Tierstered. One of my goals is to use multiple senses when describing environments. So, I write "SOUND • SMELL • FEEL" across the top of my GM playbook. During some homefront sessions, I've struggled to engage Blodwen's instinct of Preservation ("to protect the natural world"). So, I add this to my prep: Play them against each other: Glennys objects to Blodwen about Rhianna's plans for palisade.

847.title: Don't over-prep!
847.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Between sessions › Prep › Don't over-prep!
847.body: Remember why you prep: to give yourself cool stuff to say, to speed up play, to shore up weaknesses, to hone your craft, and to give yourself permission to play hard and inflict real consequences. With that in mind, prep only: - 1) What you need to prep , like stats for a monster the PCs will fight, or a love letter to a player who missed a session - 2) What you can't easily improvise , like a map, a threat's impending doom, or a complex political web - 3) What you want to prep , because it brings you joy, reduces stress, or helps you meet a goal or grant a player's wish Specifically, resist the urge to prep a plot or every potential path the PCs might take. Prep what you need, what you can't improvise, and what helps you be your best. Then play to find out what happens.

848.title: Check-ins
848.body: Play with any group long enough, and you're bound to have issues. Players who get on each other's nerves. Mismatched expectations. Disruptive behaviors. Hurt feelings. Miscommunications. Etc. When there's a player-level issue, talk to them about it. Don't let things fester, and for Tor's sake don't try to "fix" the issue with game mechanics or fictional "consequences" (punishments). There is no ingame solution for a player-level problem. Stonetop includes tools to facilitate this kind of communication. Content guidelines help set expectations and boundaries. Reviewing them at the start of each session (see opposite page) keeps them top-of-mind. Calling "time out" gives you a way to course correct mid-session. And the End of Session move invites players to ask for things to change. (And if your group finds other communication or safety tools to be helpful, then by all means, use them!) Some issues, though, are best handled with a check-in: outside of a session, one-on-one or with only the relevant players. Maybe you do this in person, right after a session, or over a drink, or on a walk, or whatever. Maybe it's a call or an online chat. Pick an approach that feels comfortable for all involved. Encourage your players to initiate check-ins, too, with you and with each other. If they have a concern, the best thing to do is talk about it. If you suspect that a player is unhappy, then let them know what you've noticed and give them space to respond. "Hey, you seemed uncomfortable during that scene with Iwan. Did we cross a line there?" See where the conversation goes. Sometimes, it's unrelated to the game, or you misread the situation, or they just don't want to talk about it. When a player says that they're unhappy with something about the game, listen . Ask for clarification and specifics, but don't argue or defend yourself. Thank them, and ask them what they'd like to see change. Can you make or facilitate that change? If so, great—do it. If you can't, or don't want to, that's okay too. But be honest about it. Have a calm conversation about why. Look for a compromise that works. If a player is causing trouble, then point out what they're doing. Focus on behaviors and impact, not personality. "When you keep canceling last-minute, I can't prep effectively. Or I have to prep more, in case you're not there." Say what you'd like to see change. Ask if they're able to make that change, and how they plan to do so. Try to find a solution if you can. Sometimes, the solution is "stop playing together," or to stop playing Stonetop and play something else. That's okay—you and your players are more important than the game! But you'll never figure that out without talking to your players. So check in with each other, often. ^check-ins

849.title: Starting a session
849.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session
849.body: Folks arrive at a session carrying their week with them—work, kids, traffic, etc. Give everyone a chance to settle. Chat and catch up. Let the noise of the world fade a bit before you start to play. Have a checklist of things to do at the start of each session. Once everyone is settled, start to work through that list. This serves as a ritual. You're drawing a magic circle and stepping back into the fictional world you've made together. Each group's checklist will be different, but common items include:

850.title: Logistics
850.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Logistics
850.body: What are your plans for food and drink? Who's taking notes? When do folks need to leave? When should you take breaks? Etc. Set timers as needed. Be sure to save time for the End of Session move!

851.title: Recap
851.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Recap
851.body: Remind everyone what happened last time, and where things left off. If possible, get the players to do this themselves. Ask the notetaker to give a recap, or ask players to take turns doing so. Add details and make corrections as needed, but don't nitpick. Listen to what your players say—it's a good indicator of what's important to them!

852.title: Content guidelines
852.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Content guidelines
852.body: As a group, read the "Content" section on your steading playbook. If you think this session might bump against these guidelines, say so now. Ask if anyone wants to add or change something. Remind folks to call "time out" if a guideline is broken, if they need a break, or if they want to talk things over. See Discuss tone for guidance.

853.title: Highlighting
853.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Highlighting
853.body: Go over stuff that you want everyone to keep in mind or at least be aware of. This might include: - PC and/or follower instincts - Relationship changes from the end of last session - Outstanding wishes - Goals you're working on, that you'd like the players to help you with Customize this list to match your needs and preferences. Not every group highlights the same things.

854.title: Bookkeeping
854.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Bookkeeping
854.body: Have players update their playbooks and inserts if needed. Like, if they've had a few days at home since the last session, have them reset HP and clear debilities. If someone Leveled Up, make sure they've updated their XP and marked a new move. That sort of thing.

855.title: Starting moves, questions, love letters
855.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Starting moves, questions, love letters
855.body: If any PCs have moves that trigger at the start of session (like the Would-be Hero's "Destined" background), or if you've written setup questions or love letters, do them now. Let them serve as a final shift into actual play.

856.title: Opening scene(s)
856.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Starting a session › Opening scene(s)
856.body: Address one or more PCs. Say (or ask) where they are, who they're with, what they're up to. Describe the environment, ask some questions. Portray NPCs. Make a soft GM move, something that propels the PC into action or conflict. Ask, "What do you do?" Play to find out what happens .

857.title: Pacing
857.body: Stonetop tends to be slower-paced than many RPGs. It has its moments of drama and intense action. But it also encourages you to dwell on the details of daily life and to spend time chewing the scenery. Finding the right pace and rhythm for your group is important.

858.title: Adventures vs. homefront
858.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Pacing › Adventures vs. homefront
858.body: A key tension in the game is how much time to spend on adventures (expeditions and crises) vs. homefront play. The game won't always tell you when it's time to switch, and that's intentional. Use your players' wishes from the End of Session to calibrate the pace of the game. If the players are itching for adventure, nudge them towards letting the Seasons Change, or offer an opportunity to make progress towards some goal. If they want more downtime but the Seasons Change and a threat worsens or makes itself known, maybe telegraph a threat that they can't do anything about right now (like growing unrest in Marshedge or some fell beast spotted in the Flats). The game mechanics will sometimes drive play in a particular, unavoidable way. If the Seasons Change and threats abound, then there's no way folks are getting a quiet winter. But maybe you can give each PC one pleasant day before the trouble starts? If your players want to constantly work on projects or head off on multiple adventures each season, don't be afraid to pump the brakes. Remind them of their obligations, and if they ignore them, start to oppose their wishes. Tell them the consequences of rushing off on a half-baked expedition with no chance of success. Etc.

859.title: Increasing the pace
859.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Pacing › Increasing the pace
859.body: If you're not getting as much done in a session as you and your players would like, then try some of these: Skip the familiar. If you've played out trips on the West Road before, you don't need another montage. Say, "Four days later, you see the smoke from Gordin's Delve," and move on. Zoom out. Resist the instinct to play out every interaction or decision, every setback, every room of a site. Establish the PCs' general intentions, then gloss over the obvious, the trivial, the stuff that's just color. Resolve a whole course of action with a single move and some description. Frame scenes aggressively. Start late, end early. Have a purpose for every scene you frame—a question to answer, a conflict to resolve, something to convey, etc.—and cut away as soon as that purpose is met. Use love letters to set up strong opening scenes. Push towards resolution. Resist the urge to complicate, escalate, or otherwise draw out a situation. Have foes flee or surrender when the fight turns against them. When you get the chance to make a hard GM move, end the current situation and push the PCs into a new one. Don't put them in a spot when you can capture them. Don't pick a lesser success when they can succeed with a cost and move on. Prevent swirl. If the players start to spin their wheels, intervene. Help them identify what they want to accomplish, rather than keep talking about what they might do. Then lay out some ways to accomplish their goals. If they keep swirling, make a GM move that forces them to act.

860.title: Advancement
860.body: To start, PCs earn enough XP to Level Up every 2-4 sessions. It takes more sessions to Level Up as PCs advance—the XP cost increases, and players roll 6- less often. A PC can Level Up only at home, which encourages players to return to Stonetop once they've accumulated enough XP. PCs can also Burn Brightly at this point, which lets them exchange excess XP for success (or fewer complications).

861.title: Slowing the rate
861.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Advancement › Slowing the rate
861.body: Ideally, PCs should Level Up only once between adventures. You want players to see each new move in play before they gain another. If your players often Level Up multiple times between adventures, consider some of the following. Reset XP to 0 on Level Up. The simplest tweak, but some players might hate it. It also undercuts XP incentives once a PC has enough to Level Up. Increase the XP requirement. The default requirement is 6 + (2 × level). Increase 6 to a 10 to add an extra session to most levels. Multiply their current level by 3 instead of 2 to really slow the rate of advancement at higher levels. Adjust the End of Session questions. Be stricter about what counts as a "Yes," or rewrite questions to be harder to achieve. For example, "Did we learn more about the world or its history?" could become "Did we unearth any secrets of the past?" Let players always Burn Brightly , even if they lack the XP to Level Up. (If you have a Would-be Hero with the Driven background, you'll need to tweak that move.)

862.title: Higher-level play
862.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Advancement › Higher-level play
862.body: Past level 6, PCs become increasingly competent and harder to challenge. They have better stats, more ways of generating advantage, and lots of tools for overcoming challenges and mitigating dangers. They can start to feel invincible. To continue challenging higher-level PCs: Up the stakes. Use foes with messy attacks and moves that inflict lasting harm. Don't just threaten the harvest, threaten Stonetop itself. Don't just threaten the village, threaten the region as a whole. Make 6- results hurt. High-level PCs roll low less often, so go hard when they do. Capture them. Reveal a betrayal. Destroy a prized possession. Sever a limb. Kill a loved one. Be irrevocable. Be aggressive. Keep making soft GM moves, any chance you get. Make moves that affect multiple PCs. Make moves that force them to react before they can do what they want. Hit them where they're weak. Look for ways to make them roll their lower stats. Threaten vulnerable NPCs and resources. Make them choose where to focus their efforts , and make those choices matter. Present threats on multiple fronts. Whatever they don't deal with should escalate, go poorly, or have repercussions. But also—and this is important— let them enjoy their wins . Give them chances to steamroll challenges that previously would have given them pause. Celebrate their competence and their accomplishments. Then, when you do challenge them, it'll feel all the more dire.

863.title: Retiring PCs
863.body: A PC might exit play because: - They die and pass through the Last Door - They retire after a lasting injury, as per Convalesce - Surplus runs short in winter , and the weariest PC's player has them die, leave, or retire - They cross the line from protagonist to villain Descent into villainy - Their player decides to have them leave Stonetop or retire from adventuring - Their player leaves the game

864.title: Becoming a threat
864.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Retiring PCs › Becoming a threat
864.body: When a PC descends into villainy and monstrosity, they stop being a PC and become a threat under the GM's control. Write them up as both a threat and a monster , using their established moves, possessions, and abilities as inspiration. Don't worry about replicating their playbook abilities exactly, and feel free to make them scarier than they were as a PC.

865.title: Retirement
865.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Retiring PCs › Retirement
865.body: If a PC retires from play but stays in the village or the region, then turn them into an NPC. By default, their portrayal falls to you. But if everyone agrees, their original player can portray them. Whoever portrays them, don't let them overshadow the PCs. They're retired! It's okay for them to advise and support the PCs, or to help meet the requirements of a plan. But they shouldn't act as followers or solve the PCs' problems. If need be, ask the ex-PC's original player why they can't or won't help with any given problem.

866.title: Epilogues
866.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Retiring PCs › Epilogues
866.body: When a PC retires or passes away, give them an epilogue: a scene or two to send them off and say goodbye. If the PC died, play out the funeral or wake. Establish the local traditions, if you haven't already. Show how NPCs react. Ask the surviving PCs to say some words. Let the dead PC's player savor the farewell to their fallen alter ego. If the PC leaves Stonetop, then have the player narrate their departure, as well as a glimpse of their future—a "Where are they now?" moment. On the road to Lygos, wandering among the Hillfolk, stepping on the Fae paths to never be seen again… That sort of thing. If the PC retires, play out their decision to do so. Then, now or later, have the player describe a typical day in their new life. Who are they with and what are they up to? How do they seem to be getting on? What do they seem to be enjoying? What regrets weigh them down? Invite the other players to contribute, by asking questions, suggesting details, or playing out their own scenes of grief and loss, of absence and new beginnings. Epilogues should skip game mechanics. These aren't scenes with conflict or even stakes. They're just vignettes, meant to give a sense of closure to a PC's tale.

867.title: New PCs
867.body: You might need to introduce a new PC to an ongoing game when: - A new player joins your group - A PC dies, retires, or otherwise leaves play (see previous page), and the player creates a replacement PC If you're adding a player to an ongoing game, then brief them on the game so far. Have them read the Setting Overview and Moves & Gear handouts. Ideally, have them read the group's session logs and look over the steading playbook. Schedule time to answer any questions they have.

868.title: Creating the character
868.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › New PCs › Creating the character
868.body: Have the player pick one of the unused playbooks. They should also pick a background that reflects either a new arrival in Stonetop or a local NPC stepping up to PC status—each playbook has at least two such backgrounds. You don't want this new, highly-skilled PC to have been here all along without ever getting involved. When the player gets to the "worldbuilding" part of their playbook (back page, left column), help them make choices that fit with the established lore of your game. As a rule, new PCs start play at level 1, no matter how seasoned everyone else is. Don't worry about "balance." A level 1 PC in Stonetop is plenty competent, and starting fresh lets the player learn the playbook and develop their character through play. That said: it's okay to start a new PC at a higher level if there's a compelling reason (like the PC is an ancient hero freed from stasis), the player is familiar with Stonetop , and the whole group is cool with it.

869.title: Introductions
869.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › New PCs › Introductions
869.body: Have the player stop at the Introductions part of their playbook. Decide together how you'll bring them into the game. There are two basic approaches: Play it out . Introduce the new PC in play. Frame a scene with them arriving in town, or have them be there when the PCs get home from an adventure. Or, like, have that clumsy kid get struck by lightning during a crisis and become the Stormmarked Heavy just in time to save the day. Skip the formal Introductions on their playbook, but ask questions as needed. This approach can be fun and visceral, but it can also take up a lot of game-time and inadvertently set up PC-PC conflicts. Time skip . The new PC appears (or steps up) between sessions, during a stretch of downtime. Establish that they've been here (or been active) a while. Have the new PC do Introductions more or less normally at the start of the next session (or between sessions, if that works for your group). Maybe write a love letter to each existing PC that both covers events during the time skip and that establishes their connection to this new PC.

870.title: Getting them involved
870.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › New PCs › Getting them involved
870.body: Once they're in play, make sure the new PC matters. Prepare hooks that touch on their background, instincts, or unresolved questions. Work their worldbuilding elements into the game. Give them a little more spotlight, and allow them to find their place in the game. Handled well, the introduction of a new PC is not disruptive. It deepens and adds new texture to your game.

871.title: Conflict between PCs
871.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Conflict between PCs
871.body: One of your players' agendas is to portray a compelling character , and such characters won't always agree. That's okay. Actually, that's great! Handled well, conflict between PCs can be high drama and some of the most memorable parts of the game. Most PC conflicts are just disagreements, or one PC trying to stop another PC from doing something rash. The Would-be Hero goes to charge the corrupted boar, but the Marshal Interferes and stops them. The Blessed wants to subdue the beast and heal it if she can, and Persuades the other PCs to let her try. Encourage players to argue in character, not to "win" but to establish their positions and their values. Use moves like Persuade (vs. PCs), Interfere, and Seek Insight to keep things moving.

872.title: Intractable differences
872.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Conflict between PCs › Intractable differences
872.body: Over time, disagreements can grow into ongoing conflicts. The Judge won't stand for the Fox's deceit. The Fox can't forgive the Heavy for his husband's death. The Lightbearer frets over the state of the Seeker's soul. When you first sense such tensions, check in with the players. Make sure that the frustrations and emotions are felt by the characters and not the players. If the players are getting upset with each other, take a step back and look for a solution. Update your content guidelines, fix the fiction if you need to, and commit to whatever changes you've all agreed upon. If everyone is on board, though, then lean into the conflict. Ask folks what they're looking for. Escalation? Resolution? Heartbreak? Use their answers to guide what comes next.

873.title: Open conflict
873.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Conflict between PCs › Open conflict
873.body: When PCs directly oppose each other through violence, subterfuge, sabotage, or whatever—then slow down. A lot. First, do a temperature check. If things feel heated between the players, then call time out. Check in. Make sure everyone wants to proceed. A little conversation can go a long way towards preventing hurt feelings, resentment, and distrust. If everyone is on board with the conflict, then give everyone involved a chance to declare their intent. When one PC does something that actively affects another PC or their interests, tell the targeted player what they perceive. Ask what they do in response. If any other PCs are present or involved, ask what they do as well. Figure out what moves are being triggered, and in what order. Very often, someone will end up Interfering. Use the results of PC actions and player moves to inform what comes next. Keep checking in with the players. Move the spotlight and give other PCs chances to get involved. If someone rolls a 6-, push things to their logical (if unfortunate) conclusion, or interrupt the whole conflict with some unrelated but pressing problem.

874.title: Descent into villainy
874.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Conflict between PCs › Descent into villainy
874.body: Sometimes, a PC starts down a dark path. Maybe they keep using a major arcanum and racking up consequences. Maybe they are corrupted by the Things Below. Maybe they die but refuse to pass on, and linger as a Ghost , Revenant , or Thrall . Maybe they just make one grim choice after another. It's usually okay for a PC to be wayward or wicked, but not both. If a PC schemes, lies, and causes trouble for the other PCs but their heart is in the right place, that's interesting. And if a PC relies on foul magic or despicable acts, but does so in alignment with the other PCs' goals, then that can be compelling. But a PC who's both evil and opposed to the other PCs is no longer a protagonist. They're a villain. When a PC crosses the line into villainy, or just cozies up to it, check in. Does this PC's player think they can be redeemed? Do the other players want to try? If not, does everyone want to play out one last conflict or scheme? If the answer to any of these is "No," then the PC should become a threat . If the answer to all three questions is "Yes," then focus on that confrontation in an upcoming session. If the party fails to redeem their one-time ally, then that PC becomes a threat.

875.title: Regarding secrets
875.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Conflict between PCs › Regarding secrets
875.body: When PCs come into conflict, it's natural that they will start lying to each other, or keeping secrets. Great! More drama! But don't let players keep secrets from each other. Don't accept notes they pass you. Don't have private chats or huddles in the hall. Encourage everyone to keep in mind what they know vs. what their character knows, and then enjoy the dramatic irony.

876.title: Time skips
876.body: Stonetop often has you gloss over stretches of time. You might cover days, weeks, or months of travel or downtime with little more than a brief description and a few questions for the PCs. But sometimes, you want to zoom way out and jump farther forward in time. Maybe you reach the end of a story arc and you'd like to let things breathe , or let the PCs age and mature. Perhaps your group takes a break for real-world reasons, and you want to reflect that in the fiction. A PC might head off to Lygos and you want to jump ahead to their return. Or maybe there's a big shake-up and you want to skip the immediate aftermath. How you handle a time skip depends largely on its length.

877.title: Months, seasons, years
877.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Time skips › Months, seasons, years
877.body: If you're skipping a few months, seasons, or years, then write love letters to cover the gap. These can be fairly involved, including not only a roll and its results, but also invitations to narrate events and/or to involve the other players. "Dear Marshal, it's been a peaceful year but tell us—what trouble has stemmed from having a standing watch that you never predicted? Then, ask each of the other PCs one of the following …" If the time skip straddles the change of just one season, have the players make the Seasons Change move and incorporate its results into your letters. For a skip that spans multiple seasons, work changes to steading elements (Fortunes, Surplus, improvements, etc.) into the love letters. Either way, prep a new adventure to bring things back with a bang.

878.title: Up to a decade or two
878.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Time skips › Up to a decade or two
878.body: If you're skipping more than a few years, up to a decade or two, take a session off from actual play. Instead, discuss: - How long, exactly, are you skipping? - Who wants to continue playing their current PC? How might they change? - Who wants to make new PCs? - What NPCs and setting elements should continue to be important? - How has the setting changed? Have anyone who wants to play a new PC create them now, picking any background that makes sense given the time skip. Do Introductions as normal, with the existing PCs each taking turns during steps 6-7 (PC connections). Pick a starting season and do the Seasons Change move. Use the result to craft a First Adventure , writing love letters to existing PCs to help fill in the skipped-over time.

879.title: Generations
879.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Time skips › Generations
879.body: If you jump ahead generations, then you're starting a whole new game. Hold a new first session . Discuss how the setting has changed in light of the game that just ended. Make and introduce new PCs, keeping those changes in mind. Ask questions about the timeline between the prior game and this one. Have spring burst forth, and prep a first adventure. In play, continue to ask questions and fill in the timeline between games. Sprinkle this game with connections to the prior one. Encourage players to do the same. Revisit the ruin where the previous Heavy died. Reveal that this Fox's father was that kid who tagged along after the old PCs. Gasp when the Marshal anoints her crew with red pigment, to honor the scar borne by the last generation's Would-be Hero.

880.title: Shake-ups
880.body: The PCs all die, or leave Stonetop for good. The village is razed and its people scattered. A cataclysm is unleashed upon the world. Big changes like these can upend your game. Don't panic. Talk to the players about how they'd like to proceed. You have two basic options:

881.title: Option 1: End the game
881.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Shake-ups › Option 1: End the game
881.body: The right answer is often the simplest: call it quits. If the story feels complete, or the shake-up has stolen the game's momentum, then let the game end. Consider using the Denouement move if that kind of closure feels right. Take a break. Play something else for a while. You can always come back to Stonetop later, either with a whole new game or by doing a time skip and starting up a few decades or generations later.

882.title: Option 2: Pivot
882.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Shake-ups › Option 2: Pivot
882.body: Keep playing, adjusting the game to reflect the shake-up. Skip forward an appropriate amount of time (see previous page). Make any mechanical changes you need. The specifics will vary case by case. Pivots like this can be hard. They make you rethink the game's core assumptions. You have to update rules and handouts. You're basically designing a new game. But if you and your players are down for it, it can be rewarding and fun as hell. The PCs have left Stonetop to help allied Hillfolk clans in a war against their rivals. I write up a new steading playbook for the ally's war-camp, skip ahead a few months, and write love letters to establish the new situation. The next few seasons might involve stealth missions, attempts to secure resources, and pitched battles. When the war ends and the PCs head home, I'll write love letters to determine what happened while they were gone. The PCs have all died trying to stop the release of Y'aaw'kara ( Book II ). A new ice age is unleashed and the Howling Curse plagues the land. We decide to skip ahead a decade or so and start fresh with all new PCs, and discuss how Stonetop has adapted to the harsh new climate, updating its resources and potential improvements accordingly. I rewrite the Seasons Change move, update the inventory inserts and gear lists, and give everyone a custom insert for tracking the Howling Curse ( Book II ). The PCs have been exiled from Stonetop and have taken up lives as wandering adventurers. The steading playbook will no longer be relevant to them; most of the Homefront moves no longer apply, or would be much harder to trigger, so I rewrite some of the questions in the End of Session move, and update Level Up to require "a quiet stretch of downtime," instead of "a quiet stretch at home." All in all, the game will now play out more like a traditional fantasy-adventure RPG.

883.title: Ending your game
883.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Ending your game
883.body: All stories end. Maybe the story of your Stonetop burns bright and fast. Maybe it stretches on for years. Maybe it just… fizzles out. And that's fine. Not every story gets a good ending. Ideally, though, you'll see the end coming. Maybe real life forces your hand, and one or more of your players are moving, or having a kid, or starting school or a new job and they can't play anymore. Maybe you sense the game's story arcing towards its conclusion. Or maybe you or some of your players are just getting the itch to play something else.

884.title: Planning for the end
884.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › Ending your game › Planning for the end
884.body: When you see the end coming, you have an opportunity. An opportunity for climax, denouement, and a fitting farewell. A chance to give your shared story the final chapter it deserves.

885.title: 1. Schedule a planning session
885.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session
885.body: Once you realize it's time to start ending the game, schedule a planning session with your players. Expect it to take an hour or two, so it might just be part of a normal session. Try to make sure everyone can be there. If you can, give yourself some lead-time so that you and your players can prep.

886.title: 2. Prepare
886.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › 2. Prepare
886.body: Ask your players to review the questions below, and give them some thought before the planning session. If they can't attend, ask them to send you their wish lists beforehand. Encourage everyone else to come with notes, or at least thoughts. Before the game ends: - What arcs do you want resolved? - What questions do you want to see answered? - What scenes do you hope to have? - Are there any moves, artifacts, or arcana you'd love to see in action? - What goals do you want a chance for your PC to accomplish? If you have time, provide the players with a list of hanging threads for reference. That includes active threats , stuff from your "I wonder…" list “I wonder…”, unsolved mysteries and unfished projects, player wishes yet to be granted, and so forth. Give these questions some thought, yourself. You're allowed to have opinions!

887.title: 3. Assemble the wishlist
887.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › 3. Assemble the wishlist
887.body: Once you've gathered for the planning session, refer to the questions from step 2, above. Print them out if playing in person, or post them somewhere everyone can see. The goal here is to establish each player's wishlist of things they'd like to do, resolve, or see in play before the game ends. If anyone is absent and sent you their wish list, read it off now. After that, go around the group and have each player share their list. Ask for clarification if needed, but try to limit discussion. You want everyone to freely share what they want. Take notes, ideally somewhere public. Keep track of which players wished for what. If you have ideas about how you'll accomplish these things, great—maybe jot that down privately. But keep the focus on what folks want for now. If you have any of your own wishes that haven't been mentioned, share those now. Open the floor for discussion. Let folks ask questions, make suggestions, endorse other folk's wishes, and reconsider their own. If your players wish for conflicting things, talk it over and come to a compromise. If they wish for something infeasible in the time you have, try to identify what is feasible instead. Prioritize. Everyone (including you) can pick one "must-have" item, and up to two "important" items. Give folks the chance to strike off items they added earlier that they don't actually care about. Everything else is low priority, a "nice to have." To be clear: this is a wish list, not a list of promises. You're all agreeing to try and fit these things in the game, but there's no guarantee how exactly they'll play out when that happens. "Deal with Brennan & the trade embargo" just means a chance to tie off those threads. Hopefully the PCs will succeed. But they might not!

888.title: Driving toward resolution
888.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › Driving toward resolution
888.body: From now on, push play towards those must-have and important wishes. Work them into your prep. Keep the wish list handy as a reference during play, and get to the "nice to have" wishes as you can. Make use of techniques for increasing the pace . Skip over the familiar. Zoom out and gloss over stuff where you can. Frame scenes aggressively. Make GM moves that resolve situations, rather than escalating or drawing them out. Resist the urge to introduce new threads or complications unless they directly relate to an item on the wish list. Step in when the players start to swirl. Focus. You're in the endgame, now. I'm starting a new job soon, and won't be able to keep running the game. I reckon we have time for maybe four more sessions. Prior to the next session, I send out the questions and my list of hanging threads, and ask folks to create wishlists. During our planning session, our discussion results in these priorities:

889.title: MUST-HAVES
889.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › Driving toward resolution › MUST-HAVES
889.body: - Deal with Brennan & the trade embargo (Rhianna, me) - See the Mighty Servant in play (Vahid) - Tell Caradoc how I feel (Blodwen) - Prove himself to the village, once and for all (Caradoc)

890.title: IMPORTANT
890.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › Driving toward resolution › IMPORTANT
890.body: - Resolve Nia's spider connection (me, Blodwen) - Try to make peace with Owain (Rhianna) - Settle things with Maire (Vahid) - Use Big Damn Hero (Caradoc) - Have it out with Vahid (Rhianna)

891.title: NICE TO HAVE
891.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › NICE TO HAVE
891.body: - Tegwen gets her comeuppance (Blodwen, Rhianna) - Get back at Mutra (Vahid) - See Eira in retirement (Rhianna) - Morwenna's wedding (Caradoc) - Punch Brennan's face (Caradoc) If we're going to see the Mighty Servant on-screen, Vahid will need to acquire its aetherium helm and spend time with its chassis, assembling it. Dealing with Brennan and the trade embargo implies a direct confrontation. And if Caradoc is going to prove himself to the village, it should happen near Stonetop. We've got only two sessions left, so we jump to spring and I use love letters to set the scene: Morwenna's wedding (a subdued affair), Blodwen and Caradoc dancing, Brennan's goons blockading the West Road, a skeevy adventurer sneaking past to sell the aetherium helm to Vahid. During the session, Rhianna and Vahid have a row over his obsession with the Servant. The PCs and Rhianna's crew kill most of Brennan's men and drive off the rest. Caradoc uses Big Damn Hero and crosses "Would-be" off his playbook. Vahid heads north to assemble the Mighty Servant. The session ends there. For the final session, we skip ahead to summer. Another round of love letters establish that Vahid is still assembling the Servant. Blodwen's and Caradoc's romance is going well. And Brennan is marching towards Stonetop with a force of Manmarcher mercenaries, intent on beating Stonetop into submission. Will Rhianna and Caradoc rally the village? Will Blodwen convince Nia to call on the spiders for aid? Will Vahid return with the Mighty Servant and help turn the tables? Will Caradoc punch Brennan in the face? I don't know, but we'll play to find out!

892.title: The final sessions
892.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › The final sessions
892.body: Figure out when your last session will be, and how many sessions you have before then. As you prep for those final sessions, keep the wishlist forefront in your mind. Look at what you need to cover and how many sessions you have left. Use time skips , love letters , and aggressive scene-framing to get the wishes on screen. If you don't see a way to do so, talk to your players and ask for ideas. If you think you have an idea, but it involves committing the PCs to a certain course of action, check in and make sure everyone is on board. Ignore moves, procedures, and rules that don't help you hit the wish list items in the time that remains. If the last session will hinge on a battle with Yaarowslow the Many in the ruins of Vor Svetelik, then don't bother with Seasons Change or the journey there or how many supplies the PCs have left. During the finale, especially: go big and don't hold back . Make bold GM moves that force the PCs to make hard choices, take desperate risks, and put it all on the line. When they roll a 6-, make it hurt. Now more than ever, let things burn .

893.title: The end
893.breadcrumb: The Game Ongoing › 1. Schedule a planning session › The end
893.body: When the final conflict ends and the dust starts to settle, take a breath. Then wrap up the current situation to whatever level of detail makes sense. Don't use any moves or mechanics. Just describe the scene and give the players space to say what they do, what happens next. If there are moments they want to play out, let them. If they have questions, answer them. Use what time remains to do this one last move, together.

894.title: DENOUEMENT
894.body: When the game comes to an end , go around the table. On your turn, pick a scene below and ask a player or the GM to describe it. When you describe a scene , make it involve your PC, an NPC important to them, or (if you're the GM) any NPC you like. - A return home - A funeral, a farewell, an ending - A struggle, an absence, grief - A time of rebuilding, renewal, hope - A lasting change, a transition - A beginning, a fresh start, a birth - A memory, a story, a legend Go around as many times as you like. Change who you ask each time, or just pick a scene to describe yourself. When you're done, thank your friends, wish them well, and say goodbye. The end. ^denouement

895.title: Inserts
895.body: [!abstract]- Printable inserts (2nd-printing PDF, Book I pp. 141–153) ^printable-inserts-2nd-printing-pdf-book-i-pp-141-153 Your PC will have at least one insert, a double-sided half-page sheet that you can slip inside your folded playbook. PDFs are included in the playkit on the Lampblack & Brimstone website, but the inserts are also included in this chapter for reference. Every PC gets the Inventory sheet. See Gear and possessions for an explanation on how this insert is used. The back of the insert will vary based on your playbook and choices made. If you are… - … the Ranger with the animal companion move , then you get the Animal Companion insert, of course; - … the Marshal , then you get the Crew insert; - … the Blessed with the Initiate background , then you get the Initiates of Danu insert; - … the Lightbearer , then you get the Invocations insert; Otherwise, you get a generic insert with space for tracking followers and a summary of your player agenda, principles, and rules. The PDF in the playkit is set up to print with the Inventory sheet on one page and the playbook-specific (or generic follower/agenda sheets) on the back. Only one copy of each sheet is included in this chapter. There are also three inserts that can be gained as a result of the Death's Door move and bad decision-making: the Ghost , the Revenant , and the Thrall . These inserts are normally printed out double-sided on a half-sheet of paper. You take one of them only when it becomes relevant. In addition to the inserts presented in this chapter, you might also get an insert that represents a major arcanum . The Seeker starts play with a major arcanum as indicated by their background; and if you take the Heavy playbook with the "Storm-marked" background, you start play with the Storm Markings major arcanum. You'll find these in Appendix D: Major Arcana ( Book II ).

Loyalty (max 3) - [ ] Play, grooming, training, affection - [ ] Time off on its own, free to roam - [ ] Cozy quarters, comfort, ample food - [ ] \ \ \ Each time you take Beast of Legend, pick 1: - [ ] They are exceptional (see Order Followers below) - [ ] They get +4 HP and +1 armor - [ ] They develop a unique ability or trait

905.title: Order followers
905.body: When you direct them to make a move and they do so, roll +1 instead of +STAT if any of their tags apply (or +2 if they're also exceptional ). If no tags apply, roll +0. If any of their tags would get in the way, roll with disadvantage. When they are without orders or they act on their own initiative , the GM says what they do and how it goes. Strengthen your bond When you pay their cost and you haven't done so recently, they hold +1 Loyalty (max 3). Spend their Loyalty 1-for-1 to have them: - Overcome their fear to do as you say - Resist acting on their instinct/tags/traits - Do something they don't want to do (as long as it's not abhorrent or suicidal) Loyal to the end When your companion is at 0 HP , roll +0, with advantage if it holds Loyalty: on a 10+ , it'll be fine once it regains any HP; on a 7-9 , it gets the injured tag; on a 6- , it's injured and will die soon unless someone saves it. ^order-followers

Loyalty (max 3) Pick 1 on each line: he / she / they / \ \ \ — dismissed / pitied / feared / venerated — cagey / friendly but firm / imperious — rites of… iron / secret naming / winter - [ ] Gwendyl, your mentor — Herb-wise , gossipy , tireless , healer , magical HP 6; Armor 0; Damage iron knife d6 ( hand ); Instinct to take offense Moves: Tend to the sick, injured, women in labor · Weave a talisman of fertility or good luck · Point out a flaw in a person or plan Cost consideration, affection; Loyalty (max 3) Pick 1 on each line: he / she / they / \ \ \ — a big family / has taken you in / lives alone — blunt / demanding / put upon / suffers no fools — rites of… earth & soil / mourning / petition (Order Followers and Strengthen Your Bond, as on the Animal Companion insert.)

914.title: Invocations
914.body: Lightbearer, you start knowing 2 Invocations. Each time you reach an even-numbered level, learn 1 new Invocation. While one Invocation is ongoing , you can't use another. You can end an Invocation whenever you wish, and it will end immediately if your holy light is extinguished. An invocation's range is equal to that of its light source.

915.title: BATH OF HEALING LIGHT
915.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › BATH OF HEALING LIGHT
915.body: Cup your hands around your light and focus it. Your patient... (pick 2): - Regains 5 HP (can pick this twice) - Clears a debility (can pick this twice) - Has one of their problematic wounds stabilized - Recovers from a minor condition (drunk, etc.) Reduced : pick only 1 (instead of 2). Empowered : add these to your possible choices: - Regains 10 HP (can pick this twice) - Fully recovers from a problematic wound - Is cured of a dire affliction, poison, or disease

916.title: BLINDING LIGHT
916.body: (ongoing) Your light blazes. Any in range who look at it are temporarily blinded. Those not looking at it directly must avert their eyes. You are unaffected. Reduced : the light flares only for a moment. Empowered : if you wish, your allies are unaffected.

917.title: CLEANSING LIGHT
917.body: Your light flares, dispelling magical effects within range. Potent, lasting magics are merely suppressed, and slowly return to power once removed from your light. Reduced : potent, lasting magics are unaffected; other magical effects are merely suppressed. Empowered : the invocation is ongoing ; while it lasts, any magical effects created in or brought into range are dispelled/suppressed.

918.title: COLD LIGHT OF DAY
918.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › COLD LIGHT OF DAY
918.body: (ongoing) All in your light appears as it really is, without the benefit of illusion, shapeshifting, or disguise. Reduced : it lasts only a few moments, just long enough to glimpse the truth. Empowered : illusions in the light are dispelled and shapeshifters in the light are momentarily stunned.

919.title: DANCING LIGHT
919.body: (ongoing) Your light takes to the air, floating as you direct it, untethered from its fuel. You can move it anywhere that you can see it, and even change its shape or color. Reduced : it dims, reducing its range by one step. Empowered : you can use another Invocation through the Dancing Light while it is ongoing .

920.title: GO BACK TO THE SHADOW
920.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › GO BACK TO THE SHADOW
920.body: Spirits of darkness in your light take 2d8 damage (ignores armor). Roll damage for each spirit separately. A spirit reduced to 0 HP is either banished from this world or back to whatever tethers it here. Reduced : affected spirits take only 1d8 damage. Empowered : a spirit reduced to 0 HP is either utterly destroyed OR it's banished from the world and anything tethering it here is destroyed (GM's choice).

921.title: HOLD BACK THE DARKNESS
921.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › HOLD BACK THE DARKNESS
921.body: (ongoing) Spirits and creatures of darkness are repelled by your light and cannot approach. The cowardly or mindless flee outright. Those forced into range of your light deal damage with disadvantage. Reduced : you must maintain an unbroken litany of prayers in order to maintain the effect. Empowered : affected entities that are forced into range of your light are vulnerable to mundane weapons, their supernatural defenses suppressed.

922.title: MOTH TO A FLAME
922.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › MOTH TO A FLAME
922.body: (ongoing) Name an individual or type of mortal creature. They gaze raptly at your light and will follow it, slowly. The effect ends if they take damage. Reduced : it lasts only briefly OR only some of the named creatures are affected (GM's choice). Empowered : the effect continues for a few moments after they first take damage. Taking damage a second time ends the effect immediately.

923.title: TERRIBLE AS THE DAWN
923.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › TERRIBLE AS THE DAWN
923.body: (ongoing) Name an individual or type of mortal creature. Your light fills them with dread, causing them to recoil and back away. The cowardly flee outright. Reduced : all mortal creatures but you are affected, including your allies. Empowered : even the brave must cower or flee.

924.title: WARMTH OF THE SUN
924.breadcrumb: Inserts › Invocations › WARMTH OF THE SUN
924.body: (ongoing) While in your light, you and your allies are free of fear and doubt, and unharmed by extreme cold. Reduced : only one person in the light is protected. Empowered : the light also protects from necromantic and life-draining effects.

925.title: Followers
925.body: (The generic insert: follower-tracking sheets plus a player reference.) Two blank follower blocks, each with: Name, Tags, HP, exceptional / group checkboxes, Max. HP, Armor, Damage, Instinct, Moves (three lines), Cost, Loyalty (max 3), Gear (◇ and ◇◇ lines), and Notes.

926.title: Player's Agenda
926.body: - Portray a compelling character - Engage with the fictional world - Play to find out what happens Player's Principles - Begin and end with the fiction - Connect with the other PCs - Show us what's important to you - Have goals and pursue them - Be bold, take risks - Embrace difficulty, setback, and failure - Participate in worldbuilding - Build on what others have said - Give others a chance to shine - Participate in the conversation When in doubt… - Visualize the situation - Ask the GM for clarification - What do you want? What's your goal? - Consider your strengths and weaknesses - Look to others for ideas - Go with the obvious choice, the interesting choice, the meaningful choice... not always the "right" choice (remember, you get XP on a miss!) Triggering moves If you want to do it, then do it in the fiction. Tell us how you do it, what it looks like. Be specific. But remember: if you do it in the fiction, then you have to do it. "I rush past the hagr to grab the glowing sword" and the GM says that's Defy Danger with DEX. It's okay to say, "Oh, really? I guess I don't do that." But if you want to rush past the hagr, make with the dice. Hold and spend When a move says, "hold X Currency (until/while/so long as \ \ )," then note the Currency you hold and spend it as described by the move. Spending held Currency usually means you just do it, no roll required. Sometimes, though, spending held Currency will allow you to trigger a move (and thus roll) when otherwise you just couldn't have done it. ^players-agenda

927.title: Ghost
927.body: When you die but your soul lingers , refusing to pass through the Last Door, you gain this insert, and choose 1 Consequence (see below).

928.title: Instinct
928.body: Lose the Instinct from your playbook, and replace it with one of these (your choice): - [ ] DENIAL — To refuse to accept that you are dead. - [ ] OBSESSION — To pursue your Terrible Purpose no matter what. - [ ] ENNUI — To bemoan your condition, to wish for release.

929.title: Moves
929.body: You gain all of the following:

930.title: UNLIVING
930.body: (granted) You do not breathe. You need not eat nor drink nor sleep. You do not heal normally. You gain no benefit from magical healing, Make Camp, Recover or Convalesce.

931.title: DISEMBODIED
931.body: (granted) Your body is dead and gone, but you persist as a spirit. You can be harmed only by silver, salt, or that which harms spirits or ghosts. You normally go unseen, with the barest influence on the material world and a dim, distorted sense of reality. When you manifest a ghostly presence in shadows or darkness , the world becomes clear and pick 1. For each additional option you pick, lose 1d4 HP: - You appear solid and whole, much as you did in life - You can speak clearly and intelligibly - Your touch (or ghostly weapons) can harm the living (ignores armor) You remain manifest for as long as you concentrate; pain or shock or direct sunlight threaten your concentration, for sure.

932.title: TETHERED
932.body: (granted) Choose something to which you are bound: your mortal remains, the place where you died, an object of personal significance, etc. When you are reduced to 0 HP , mark a consequence (see below) and your essence disperses until the next sunset. You reform near your tether with half your max HP. If your tether has been destroyed, mark the Final Consequence.

933.title: Terrible Purpose
933.body: Choose 1: - [ ] LONGING Name the person or persons you refuse to let go of. When you spend the night watching them , regain all your HP or clear all your debilities. When they genuinely return your affections , free of fear or horror, either regain all your HP and clear your debilities, or clear a consequence (see below). When they rebuff you or recoil from you , mark a consequence (see below). When they die peacefully and pass through the Last Door , so do you. Should they be taken from you violently , mark the Final Consequence (see below). - [ ] VENGEANCE Name the person or persons who must pay. When you spend the night wailing, howling, and raging in a lonely place , regain all your HP or clear all your debilities. When you make one of them pay and ensure that they know why , either regain all your HP and clear your debilities, or clear a consequence (see below). When they defeat or escape you , mark a consequence (see below). When you kill the last of them , pass through the Last Door. Should they die before you're finished with them , mark the Final Consequence (see below). - [ ] DUTY Name the task you refuse to leave undone. When you spend the night working on your task , regain all your HP or clear all your debilities. When you achieve a significant milestone towards your task , either regain all your HP and clear your debilities, or clear a consequence (see below). When you fail to perform your task or suffer a material setback , mark a consequence (see below). When the task is finally complete , pass through the Last Door. Should the task become impossible to perform , mark the Final Consequence (see below).

934.title: Ghost · Consequences
934.body: When you first take this insert, choose 1. Choose another whenever a move tells you to do so. - [ ] BODYSNATCHER When you possess an unconscious or willing person , lose 1d4 HP and control their actions. Use your stats and moves for any actions they take while under your control. When you force your host to do something contrary to their instinct or something that they find abhorrent , roll +CHA: on a 10+ , they do it anyway; on a 7-9 , they pick 1: - They resist that action, and you'll never be able to force them to do it or anything similar, but you otherwise remain in control - They do it, but it costs you—lose 2d4 HP On a 6- , they force you from their body and you mark a consequence. You'll never be able to possess them again. When your host takes damage , you lose an equal amount of HP. If your host dies while you still have HP, you can keep them alive until your possession ends. - [ ] BREAKDOWN You lash out in an unthinking, unfeeling rage that lasts until the next sunrise. Ask the GM what snippets you remember. - [ ] UNSTABLE (Requires Breakdown) You are prone to episodes of unthinking, unfeeling rage (as per Breakdown). When you roll a 6- , the GM can choose to have you enter such a rage. - [ ] DISTURBING Your presence (even unseen) disturbs beasts and children. The air around you is notably cooler, especially when you manifest. When you use intimidation and your disturbing presence to Persuade , you have advantage. - [ ] OTHERWORLDLY When you manifest a ghostly form , strange things happen: statues weep, rocks bleed, plants wither, water pools on ceilings, etc. - [ ] POLTERGEIST (Fury tracker: 4) When you get angry , lose 1d4 HP and hold that much Fury. Spend Fury, 1-for-1, to: - Shatter, break, or destroy a number of small mundane objects, or one big object. - Hurl an object at someone and roll +DEX: on a 10+ , deal your damage ( forceful ); on a 7-9 , deal your damage ( forceful ) but lose 1d4 HP. - Attack someone with telekinetic force and roll +INT: on a 10+ , fling them to a place you can see and pin them there, spending 1 HP each time they make a committed effort to break free; on a 7-9 , as 10+, but you also lose 1d4 HP. - [ ] QUARRY The Pale Hunter has caught your scent. Expect a visit, soon. - [ ] SPECTER When you terrify a living person, someone who is unconnected to your Terrible Purpose , regain 1d8 HP or clear a debility of your choice. - [ ] The Final Consequence Your tenuous connection to humanity is lost and you become a monster under the GM's control.

935.title: Revenant
935.body: When you die but cling stubbornly to your body , refusing to pass through the Last Door, you gain this insert, and choose 1 Consequence (see below).

936.title: Instinct
936.body: Lose the Instinct from your playbook, and replace it with one of these (your choice): - [ ] DENIAL — To refuse to accept that you are dead. - [ ] OBSESSION — To pursue your Terrible Purpose no matter what. - [ ] ENNUI — To bemoan your condition, to wish for release.

937.title: Moves
937.body: You gain all of the following:

938.title: UNLIVING
938.body: (granted) You do not breathe. You need not eat nor drink nor sleep. You do not heal normally. You gain no benefit from magical healing, Make Camp, Recover or Convalesce.

939.title: UNDYING
939.body: (granted) Neither poison nor disease do you harm. You feel little pain. When you take damage from cutting, stabbing, or crushing , take half damage (after armor, rounded up). When you are reduced to 0 HP , roll +CON: on a 10+ , regain half your max HP and choose 1; on a 7-9 , regain half your max HP and choose 2; on a 6- , either regain 1 HP and all 3 apply, or give up this insert and gain the Ghost insert instead. - Mark a consequence (see below) - You're out of the action until the next sunset - Your body is permanently maimed in some way of the GM's choosing If your body is completely destroyed (burnt to ash, ground to jelly, etc.), treat it as if you were reduced to 0 HP and rolled a 6-.

940.title: IMPLACABLE
940.body: (granted) When you push the limits of your undead body , lose 1d4 HP and choose 1: - Perform a feat of inhuman strength - Act with uncanny speed and grace - Refuse to be moved, held back, or knocked off course

941.title: Terrible Purpose (Revenant)
941.body: Choose 1: LONGING , VENGEANCE , or DUTY — identical to the Ghost's Terrible Purpose options above.

942.title: Revenant · Consequences
942.body: When you first take this insert, choose 1. Choose another whenever a move tells you to do so. - [ ] BREAKDOWN You lash out in an unthinking, unfeeling rage that lasts until the next sunrise. Ask the GM what snippets you remember. - [ ] UNSTABLE (Requires Breakdown) You are prone to episodes of unthinking, unfeeling rage (as per Breakdown). When you roll a 6- , the GM can choose to have you enter such a rage. - [ ] CARRION STENCH You are followed always by a horrible odor. Natural beasts will shun you; even predators will avoid you and your companions. - [ ] DEATHLY VISAGE It's clear to all who look upon you that you are dead. When you use intimidation and your sinister appearance to Persuade , you have advantage. - [ ] HOME TO VERMIN Bugs, moths, and other vermin have taken up residence in your corpse. They will do you favors if you ask. Treat them as followers: group , tiny , gross , meek , stealthy ; HP 1 each; Instinct to get distracted; Cost: genuine affection. - [ ] NIGHTKIN You can see clearly in even absolute darkness, though you see only in black and white and red. Sunlight, alas, blinds you, and direct sunlight burns your skin. - [ ] QUARRY The Pale Hunter has caught your scent. Expect a visit, soon. - [ ] STRANGE APPETITES Pick 1: still-warm blood / dying breaths / brains / bone & marrow / rotting meat / eyes When you consume your special fare , heal damage equal to half your max HP or clear a debility. - [ ] INSATIABLE (Requires Strange Appetites) When you have the opportunity to indulge your Strange Appetites , gain advantage on your next roll if you choose to do so, or Defy Danger if you choose not to (the danger being your overwhelming desires). - [ ] The Final Consequence Your tenuous connection to humanity is lost and you become a monster under the GM's control.

943.title: Thrall
943.body: When you die but call on one of the Things Below by name and beseech them to intercede , they do. Death is averted, but you gain this insert and 1 Mark (see below).

944.title: Your master
944.body: The Thing Below that you called upon? The one that plucked your soul from the Last Door and hides you from the Pale Hunter? It owns you now. Name your master here, along with any titles that you know: \ \ \

945.title: Impulse
945.body: Ask the GM to choose 1, to represent your master's nature and will: - [ ] Stoke conflict, confusion, distrust - [ ] Erode hope/faith/honor/self-image - [ ] Hide/bury/smother things or ideas - [ ] Deprive others of what they need - [ ] Inflict harm, cruelly and unnecessarily - [ ] Desecrate/mutilate/ruin things of value - [ ] Shock/terrify/horrify others - [ ] \ \ \

946.title: Instinct
946.body: Lose the Instinct from your playbook, and replace it with one of these (your choice): - [ ] FASCINATION — To explore your powers, your master, your new existence. - [ ] RESISTANCE — To struggle against your master and cling to your humanity. - [ ] SHAME — To hide and deny your true nature.

947.title: Moves
947.body: You gain all of the following:

948.title: FAVOR
948.body: (granted · Favor tracker: 3) Your Favor starts at 0 and can go no higher than 3.
